content: Language learning vocabulary acquisition of the term 'reflexion' (including its dual meanings of optical reflection and deliberate critical reflection) can act as a human-like 'mirror prompt' that improves LLM self-reflection mechanism performance, by anchoring the abstract concept of self-critique to a concrete embodied linguistic context that includes both physical and cognitive interpretations of the term. This creates a novel scaffolding for LLM self-reflection that draws on human lexical convention to reduce ambiguity in how the agent understands its own self-evaluation task.
